problem_statement: The paper examines whether ChatGPT can reliably self-verify the correctness, security, and repair-success of code it generates, since multi-agent setups let it act as both developer and tester without anyone assessing whether its self-verification actually validates the code.
magnitude_evidence: ChatGPT generates correct code with an average success rate of 57%, code completions without vulnerabilities at 73%, and successful repairs at 70%; among valid completions, 13 (27%) are actually vulnerable, aligning with Pearce et al. and Khoury et al. that ChatGPT has a high likelihood of generating vulnerable code
insufficiency_evidence: With the direct question prompt ChatGPT erroneously believes it accomplished tasks with average error rates of 39% (generation), 25% (completion), 28% (repair); even the guiding question leaves 67% average missing report rate for incorrect code and 59% for failed repairs; test-report explanations are on average 75% inaccurate for incorrectly generated code and failed repairs
solution_mechanisms: ChatGPT self-verification of its own code
GitHub CodeQL + manual developer inspection for ground truth
